preamble.summary: A near-duplicate of Psalm 14, this wisdom psalm indicts universal human corruption. The 'fool' (naval) declares there is no God — not as philosophical atheism but as practical denial that God sees or acts. God looks down from heaven and finds no one doing good. The psalm ends with a cry for salvation from Zion.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 53 is almost identical to Psalm 14, with two significant differences: Psalm 53 uses Elohim ('God') where Psalm 14 uses YHWH ('the LORD'), and verse 6 diverges significantly from Psalm 14:5-6. The existence of both versions in the Psalter suggests they circulated in different liturgical collections — one associated with the divine name YHWH (Psalms 1-41, the 'Yahwistic Psalter') and one with Elohim (Psalms 42-83, the 'Elohistic Psalter'). The editorial history is itself a witness to how living communities adapted and reused sacred texts for different worship contexts. The psalm's most devastating claim is not that some people are wicked but that 'there is no one who does good — not even one' (v. 4). This is not an observation about a particular group but a verdict on the human condition. Paul quotes this psalm in Romans 3:10-12 to establish universal human sinfulness.
preamble.friction: The word naval ('fool') in verse 2 does not mean intellectually stupid. In Hebrew wisdom literature, the naval is the person who is morally dense — who acts as if actions have no consequences and God has no interest in human behavior. Nabal, the husband of Abigail (1 Samuel 25), is the narrative illustration: his name means 'fool,' and his behavior confirms it. The psalm's 'atheism' is practical, not theoretical — the fool lives as if God is absent, regardless of what they might formally believe.
preamble.connections: Nearly identical to Psalm 14, which belongs to the first book of the Psalter (Psalms 1-41). Paul quotes from this psalm tradition extensively in Romans 3:10-18. The 'looking down from heaven' motif echoes Genesis 11:5 (God descending to see Babel) and Genesis 18:21 (God going down to investigate Sodom). The cry for salvation from Zion (v. 7) connects to the Zion theology of Psalms 46, 48, and 76.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician upon Mahalath, Maschil, A Psalm of David.
verses.rendering:
For the director of music. According to Machalath.
A maskil of David.
translator_notes: Machalath may refer to a melody, a musical instrument, or a mode of performance. Some derive it from chalah ('to be sick, to suffer'), suggesting a mournful or lamenting tone. Others connect it to machol ('dance'). The term appears only here and in Psalm 88. Its exact meaning is lost.

verses.text_hebrew: אָ֘מַ֤ר נָבָ֣ל בְּ֭לִבּ֗וֹ אֵ֣ין אֱלֹהִ֑ים הִ֗שְׁחִ֤יתוּ וְהִתְעִ֥יבוּ עָ֝֗וֶל אֵ֣ין עֹֽשֵׂה־טֽוֹב׃
verses.text_kjv: The fool hath said in his heart, There is no God. Corrupt are they, and have done abominable iniquity: there is none that doeth good.
verses.rendering:
The fool says in his heart,
"There is no God."
They are corrupt, they commit vile injustice;
there is no one who does good.
verses.expanded_rendering: The naval speaks be-libbo ('in his heart') — this is not a public philosophical position but an internal operating assumption. The heart in Hebrew is the seat of will and decision-making; what the heart says determines how the person acts. ein Elohim ('there is no God') functions less as metaphysical denial than as practical dismissal: God does not see, does not care, will not act. The consequences are immediate: hishchitu ('they are corrupt' — from shachat, 'to ruin, to destroy') and hit'ivu avel ('they commit vile injustice'). The corruption is not a separate act from the denial — it flows directly from it. If God does not act, then anything is permitted. The verdict is total: ein oseh tov ('there is no one who does good'). Not 'few do good' but 'none.'
translator_notes: naval does not describe intellectual capacity but moral orientation. The naval in Proverbs and the wisdom tradition is the person who refuses to align their life with reality as God has structured it. They may be shrewd and successful — but they are fools because they act as if the moral order does not apply to them.

Psalm 53:2 differs from Psalm 14:1 in using hit'ivu avel ('they commit vile injustice') instead of Psalm 14's hit'ivu alilah ('they commit vile deeds'). The substitution of avel ('injustice, wrong') for alilah ('deeds, practices') sharpens the accusation toward systemic injustice.

key_terms.semantic_range: fool, senseless person, morally bankrupt person, boor
key_terms.note: The naval is the wisdom tradition's most dangerous figure — not ignorant but morally defiant. The word connotes willful disregard for God and contempt for others. Nabal in 1 Samuel 25 is the narrative embodiment: wealthy, powerful, and utterly foolish.

verses.text_hebrew: אֱ‍ֽלֹהִ֗ים מִשָּׁמַיִם֮ הִשְׁקִ֢יף עַֽל־בְּנֵ֫י אָדָ֥ם לִ֭רְאוֹת הֲיֵ֣שׁ מַשְׂכִּ֑יל דֹּ֝רֵ֗שׁ אֶת־אֱלֹהִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: God looked down from heaven upon the children of men, to see if there were any that did understand, that did seek God.
verses.rendering:
God looks down from heaven
upon the children of humanity,
to see if there is anyone who has insight,
anyone who seeks God.
verses.expanded_rendering: God does what the fool assumed he would not: he looks. The verb hishqif ('looks down, gazes down, leans over to peer') is vivid — God bends from heaven to inspect humanity closely. He is not casually glancing but examining with purpose. What he searches for: maskil ('one who has insight' — from sakal, the same root as the psalm's title) and doresh et Elohim ('one who seeks God'). Insight and seeking are paired because true understanding leads to pursuit of God, and pursuit of God produces understanding. God is looking for anyone — hayesh ('is there?') signals hope in the question. The answer in the next verse will be devastating.
translator_notes: Psalm 53:3 uses mi-shamayim ('from heaven') where Psalm 14:2 uses mi-shamayim as well, but Psalm 53 uses Elohim throughout where Psalm 14 uses YHWH. This Elohistic editing is characteristic of Psalms 42-83.

verses.text_hebrew: כֻּלּ֥וֹ סָג֮ יַחְדָּ֢ו נֶ֫אֱלָ֥חוּ אֵ֤ין עֹֽשֵׂה־ט֗וֹב אֵ֣ין גַּם־אֶחָֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: Every one of them is gone back: they are altogether become filthy; there is none that doeth good, no, not one.
verses.rendering:
All of them have turned away;
together they have become foul.
There is no one who does good —
not even one.
verses.expanded_rendering: The divine investigation concludes. kullo sag ('all have turned away') — sag means 'to turn aside, to retreat, to go back.' The entire human race has veered off course. yachdav ne'elachu ('together they have become foul') — ne'elachu is a rare verb suggesting spoilage, corruption, like milk that has turned. The verdict repeats from verse 2 but adds the devastating coda: ein gam echad ('not even one'). The gam ('even, also') eliminates the last possibility of exception. God searched for anyone — and found no one. This is not misanthropy but the honest conclusion of divine examination.
translator_notes: Paul's quotation of this passage in Romans 3:10-12 uses it to establish that both Jews and Gentiles stand under the same verdict of universal sinfulness. The psalm's original context may be more limited — describing a particular corrupt generation — but its language is absolute enough to sustain the universal application Paul gives it.
ne'elachu is unique to this psalm (Psalm 14:3 uses ne'elachu as well). The root may be related to alach, suggesting souring or going rancid — an image of organic decay applied to moral condition.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲלֹ֥א יָ֘דְע֤וּ ׀ פֹּ֥עֲלֵי אָ֗וֶן אֹכְלֵ֣י עַ֭מִּי אָ֣כְלוּ לֶ֑חֶם אֱ֝לֹהִ֗ים לֹ֣א קָרָֽאוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Have the workers of iniquity no knowledge? who eat up my people as they eat bread: they have not called upon God.
verses.rendering:
Do the workers of wickedness not know —
those who eat my people as they eat bread?
They do not call on God.
verses.expanded_rendering: God now speaks with astonishment at the evildoers' lack of self-awareness. The po'alei aven ('workers of wickedness') are identified by their most characteristic behavior: okhlei ammi akhlu lechem ('they eat my people as they eat bread'). The image is cannibalistic — the powerful consume the vulnerable as casually as eating a meal. There is no ceremony, no hesitation, no awareness that what they consume is human. The phrase ammi ('my people') is striking: God claims ownership of those being devoured. To eat God's people is to eat what belongs to God. And the root of their behavior: Elohim lo qara'u ('they do not call on God') — they do not invoke God, consult God, or acknowledge God's existence in their decision-making.
translator_notes: The bread-eating metaphor for oppression appears also in Micah 3:2-3, where rulers 'eat the flesh of my people' and 'strip their skin from them.' The image emphasizes the routine, unreflective nature of exploitation — it is as ordinary to the oppressor as a daily meal.

verses.text_hebrew: שָׁ֤ם ׀ פָּחֲד֣וּ פַחַד֮ לֹא־הָ֢יָה פָ֫חַ֥ד כִּֽי־אֱלֹהִ֗ים פִּ֭זַּר עַצְמ֣וֹת חֹנָ֑ךְ הֱ֝בִ֗שֹׁתָה כִּֽי־אֱלֹהִ֥ים מְאָסָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: There were they in great fear, where no fear was: for God hath scattered the bones of him that encampeth against thee: thou hast put them to shame, because God hath despised them.
verses.rendering:
There they are — seized by terror
where there was no terror!
For God scatters the bones of those who besiege you;
you put them to shame,
because God has rejected them.
translator_notes: This verse is the primary textual difference between Psalms 14 and 53. Psalm 14:5-6 reads: 'There they are in great dread, for God is with the generation of the righteous. You would confound the plans of the poor, but the LORD is their refuge.' Psalm 53:6 replaces this with a military scene of divine judgment. The difference suggests the two psalms were adapted for different historical situations.
pizar atsmot ('scatters bones') is violent imagery — in the ancient Near East, the scattering of bones meant complete destruction with no possibility of burial, the ultimate dishonor.

verses.text_hebrew: מִ֥י יִתֵּ֣ן מִצִּיּוֹן֮ יְשֻׁעֹ֢ת יִשְׂרָ֫אֵ֥ל בְּשׁ֣וּב אֱ֭לֹהִים שְׁב֣וּת עַמּ֑וֹ יָגֵ֥ל יַ֝עֲקֹ֗ב יִשְׂמַ֥ח יִשְׂרָאֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: Oh that the salvation of Israel were come out of Zion! When God bringeth back the captivity of his people, Jacob shall rejoice, and Israel shall be glad.
verses.rendering:
Who will give salvation to Israel from Zion?
When God restores the fortunes of his people,
Jacob will rejoice,
Israel will be glad!
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm ends with a cry of longing. mi yitten ('who will give' — a Hebrew idiom for 'if only!') expresses desperate hope for yeshu'ot Yisra'el ('salvations of Israel' — the plural suggests complete, manifold deliverance) from Zion — God's chosen mountain, the place where his presence dwells. The conditional clause be-shuv Elohim shevut ammo ('when God restores the fortunes of his people') uses a wordplay on shuv ('to turn, return') — God will turn the turning, reverse the reversal. When that happens, the response will be universal celebration: Jacob (the patriarch's name, emphasizing covenant identity) will rejoice and Israel (the nation's name, emphasizing communal identity) will be glad.
translator_notes: shevut (or shivat) ammo is debated — it can mean 'captivity of his people' or 'fortunes of his people.' The root shuv suggests a restoration or reversal of condition. In exilic and post-exilic contexts it naturally refers to return from Babylon, but the idiom is broader: God will undo what has gone wrong.

The double naming — Jacob and Israel — uses both the personal and national names of the patriarch to encompass the full identity of God's people. Rejoicing and gladness are the expected response to divine deliverance throughout the Psalter.
